rationale: The citing paper gives Dhar and Wertenbroch (2012) a clear conceptual and empirical role in its theory section, summarizing their findings on how temptation contexts change total utility via self-signaling. It then explicitly states that it is building on that work to propose and test a new mechanism in charitable giving, where hedonic reference products shift the self-signal of not donating. This is used to frame hypotheses and interpret results across multiple studies, not just as background name-checking.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Dhar and Wertenbroch's self-signaling utility framework from vice-versus-virtue consumption choices to prosocial donation decisions. It argues and shows that even referenced (non-chosen) hedonic alternatives can alter self-attributions and thereby increase donation likelihood.
